coredata.dc:title: Modulating Na-ion solvation in carbonate-based electrolytes by nitrogen-doped carbon dots enables superior Na metal batteries

coredata.dc:description:
Sodium (Na) anode for Na-metal batteries (SMBs) has gained significant attention for its high theoretical capacity and the lowest redox potential. However, challenges caused by random growth of Na dendrites and unstable solid-electrolyte-interphase (SEI) layers impede the practical applications for SMBs in carbonate-based electrolytes. In this study, we propose a novel additive, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) in the carbonate-based electrolyte. NCQDs modify the solvation structures among Na-ion,
PF
6
−
anion and solvent molecules. The negatively charged NCQDs exhibit a strong affinity toward Na-ions, impeding interactions between
PF
6
−
anions and solvent molecules. This coordination between NCQDs and Na-ions slightly alters the solvation environment of the electrolyte, leading to a weak solvation structure. This electrolyte induces densely inorganic-rich SEI layers and thus improves the electrochemical performance. Our approach ensures a stable cycling of the Na metal anode for cycling up to 700 hrs at 0.5 mA cm-2 in Na||Na batteries, along with an average Coulombic efficiency (91 %) at 1 mA cm-2 in Na||Cu batteries. Moreover, practical SMBs with P2-Na0.67Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 demonstrate stable cycling stability (93 % for 110 cycles), even operating up to 4.3V. This study enables high-voltage SMBs and provides guiding principles in electrolyte design for Na-based batteries.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the Na plating/stripping behavior in the carbonate-based electrolytes containing NCQDs. In the initial deposition, negative charged NCQDs cooperate with Na-ions (Stage 1), subsequently co-plate together on the electrode (Stage 2). Upon the stripping of Na ions off (Stage 3), NCQDs are released from the electrode with Na-ions and return to repeat the subsequent cycles (e-: electron).

Characterization of NCQDs. (a) High-resolution TEM mages, (b) XPS spectra of N 1 s, and (c) FT-IR spectra of NCQDs. The insert displays the peaks corresponding to full ranges from 700 cm-1 to 4000 cm-1. (d) A schematic representation of the NCQD at the atomic level, with different types of N atoms substituted in the molecular structure labelled. (e) Zeta potential of NCQDs in water and 0.1 M NaPF6 aqueous solution. The inserted optical images depict the solutions without (left) and with (right) 0.1 M NaPF6 in NCQD aqueous solution of a concentration of 0.035 mg mL-1. (f) Optical images of the carbonate-based electrolyte without and with NCQDs. The pristine electrolyte is 1 M NaPF6 in EC/DEC (v/v, 1/1).

Characterization of electrolytes with NCQDs. (a) Summary of calculated binding energy between NCQD and a Na-ion at different binding sites. Carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sodium are marked as gray, blue, white, and purple, respectively. (b) Binding energy of Na-ion with solvent molecules. Carbon, oxygen, fluorine, hydrogen, and sodium are marked as gray, red, sky blue, white, and purple, respectively. (c) 23Na NMR spectra of various electrolytes. Enlarged 23Na NMR spectra of 1 M NaPF6 in EC/DEC/FEC (d) and DEC (e) without NCQDs (black line) and with NCQDs (red line). FT-IR spectra of the carbonyl group (C = O) in EC/DEC/FEC (f) and DEC (g) in the wavenumber range of 1800–1650 cm-1. (h) Schematic representation of the solvation structure in the diglyme and carbonate ester electrolytes with NCQDs.

Electrochemical performances of Na/Cu and Na/Na cells using 1 M NaPF6 in EC/DEC/FEC with and without NCQDs, referred to as EDF and EDFNC, respectively. (a) The CV profiles of Na||Na cells at a scan rate of 1.0 mV s-1. (b) Schematic of the electric circuit of a three-electrode cell configuration. (c) Voltage profiles of the three-electrode cell for each electrode, ECe(top), EWe(middle), EWe-Ce (bottom). (d) Voltage profiles of Na/Cu half cells at 1.0 mA cm-2 for 20 cycles after plating Na on Cu foil up to a capacity of 5 mA h cm-2. (e) The voltage-time profiles of Na/Na symmetric cells at 0.5 mA cm-2 for a fixed capacity of 0.5 mA h cm-2 (insert: detailed voltage profiles in the beginning). (f) The comparison of Na cycling stability between the proposed cell using the electrode with NCQDs and other strategies reported in previous literature.

Dendrite morphology grown on the Cu side in Na/Cu half cells. (a) Schematic diagram of Na metal plated on the Cu working electrode in the Na/Cu asymmetric cells. (b) Voltage profiles of Na||Na symmetric cells for Na plating at 10 mA cm-2. The arrows indicate the onset of dendrite formation as inferred from the voltage decay, which is represented by Sand's time (τ). SEM images of Na metal morphology according to Na plating capacities of 1, 3, and 6 mA h cm-2 at a current density of 0.5 mA cm-2 for the Na||Cu cell in EDF (c-e) and EDFNC (f-h). Cross-sectional SEM images of the electrodes are included in (e) and (f). Insets show photographs of the actual electrodes under each condition after Na-ion deposition.

Characterization of SEI layers. The in-depth XPS spectra of SEI formed on Cu electrodes after 10 cycles of Na/Cu half cells in EDF and EDFNC. The Cu electrode is etched for 30 s, which is etched for an average thickness of 2.5 nm. (a) C 1 s spectra for EDF, (b) C 1 s spectra for EDFNC, (c) N 1 s spectra for EDFNC. (d) SEI Chemical composition obtained from the XPS C 1 s, F 1 s, and N 1 s on EDF and EDFNC. The results were obtained from XPS depth profiling with Ar-ion etching time for 30 s. (e) Schematic representation of the Na electrodes with SEI layers for the electrodes according to the above results.

Electrochemical performance of Na metal full cells paired with P2-Na0.67Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 (NNM) as a cathode using EDF or EDFNC electrolyte. (a) The cycle performance at a current density of 1C (1C is 80 mA g-1.). (b) The charge/discharge voltage profiles of the 20th, 50th and 100th cycle at voltage range between 2 V and 4 V. (c) The cycle performance at a current density of 2C (160 mA g-1). (d) 2nd Charge/discharge voltage profiles of NNM electrodes in EDF and EDFNC between 2 V and 4.3 V. (e) Cyclic performance of NNM electrodes in EDF and EDFNC at 1C (150 mA g-1) between 2 V and 4.3 V.

head.ce:title: Modulating Na-ion solvation in carbonate-based electrolytes by nitrogen-doped carbon dots enables superior Na metal batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Sodium (Na) anode for Na-metal batteries (SMBs) has gained significant attention for its high theoretical capacity and the lowest redox potential. However, challenges caused by random growth of Na dendrites and unstable solid-electrolyte-interphase (SEI) layers impede the practical applications for SMBs in carbonate-based electrolytes. In this study, we propose a novel additive, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) in the carbonate-based electrolyte. NCQDs modify the solvation structures among Na-ion,
PF
6
−
anion and solvent molecules. The negatively charged NCQDs exhibit a strong affinity toward Na-ions, impeding interactions between
PF
6
−
anions and solvent molecules. This coordination between NCQDs and Na-ions slightly alters the solvation environment of the electrolyte, leading to a weak solvation structure. This electrolyte induces densely inorganic-rich SEI layers and thus improves the electrochemical performance. Our approach ensures a stable cycling of the Na metal anode for cycling up to 700 hrs at 0.5 mA cm-2 in Na||Na batteries, along with an average Coulombic efficiency (91 %) at 1 mA cm-2 in Na||Cu batteries. Moreover, practical SMBs with P2-Na0.67Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 demonstrate stable cycling stability (93 % for 110 cycles), even operating up to 4.3V. This study enables high-voltage SMBs and provides guiding principles in electrolyte design for Na-based batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As electronic devices rapidly advance and become increasingly integrated into our daily lives, lithium (Li)-based batteries power them from portable electronics to electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems [1–3]. However, limited availability of Li resources, which increases battery fabrication costs, has prompted researchers to seek alternatives beyond Li-based batteries [4]. Sodium (Na)-based batteries have emerged as a promising option due to their analogous properties to Li-based batteries, abundant natural availability of Na, and competitive cost. Among numerous anodes, Na metal stands out as an ideal candidate due to its low redox potential (−2.71 V vs. standard hydrogen electrode) and high theoretical capacity (1166 mA h g-1) [5]. This enables the realization of Na-metal batteries (SMBs) with a higher energy density compared to Na-ion batteries using hard carbon as a representative of current anodes [6,7].

Notwithstanding its valuable properties, Na metal faces challenges. It is high chemical reactivity with conventional carbonate-based electrolytes and thus continuously forms unstable solid-electrolyte-interphase (SEI) layers [8–10]. The SEI layer on the surface of Na metal triggers serious maldistribution of Na-ion flux that passes through the electrolytes, thereby engendering uneven nucleation and subsequent random mossy/dendritic Na growth. In the subsequent cycles, the SEI layer is dissolved, fractured, and reformed by the incessant growth of Na dendrites beneath it. These issues ultimately result in the depletion of the electrolyte and the generation of dead Na metal, which can cause battery failure and potential safety hazards.

Recently, numerous efforts have been devoted to investigating electrolytes that can modify the SEI layer [11–14]. This interest is motivated by the recognition of a direct correlation between the SEI layer and the electrolyte configuration/characteristics. Researchers have pursued various strategies, such as blending different types of solvents and salts [15–17], exploring diverse salt concentrations [18–20], and incorporating specialized additives [21–23]. The prevailing conclusion of these studies is that SEI layers enriched with inorganic components tend to enhance the electrochemical performance of Na metal anodes more effectively than organic-rich SEI layers. Moreover, it has also been observed that the optimal balance between the inorganic and organic constituents in SEI layers is strongly intertwined with the Na-ion solvation environment in the electrolyte [24–26]. It is therefore imperative to gain insight into and to manipulate the solvation configuration of electrolyte compositions in order to develop electrolytes that are suitable for use with Na metal.

The principal strategy to develop inorganic-rich SEI layers entails the adjustment of the coordination among electrolyte components [27–29]. This involves enhancing Na ion-anion coordination (maximizing contact ion pairs and ionic aggregates) and reducing Na ion-solvent coordination (minimizing solvent-separated ion pairs). A general approach to increase contact ion pair and ionic aggregates in the Na-ion solvation shells is to implement high-concentration electrolytes (HCEs) or incorporate non-solvated cosolvents into HCEs [19,30,31]. These methods modify the solvation degree of electrolytes and the energy level of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for anions within the solvation shell. Consequently, the anions undergo preferential reduction on the Na metal surface compared to the solvents, thereby forming inorganic-rich SEI layers. Nevertheless, the implementation of HCEs is constrained by challenges such as high viscosity, poor wetting capability, low ionic conductivity, high mass weight, and high cost. An alternative approach for weakening Na ion-solvent interaction involves improving the coordination between Na-ions and solvents by adjusting the solvent polarity and/or incorporating specific salts or additives into conventional electrolytes [32–35]. The design of novel electrolytes with their modification disrupts the interaction between Na-ions and solvents, thereby promoting coordination between Na-ions and anions. This process facilitates the formation of the inorganic-rich SEI layer on the Na metal surface.

Herein, we propose a novel solid additive, nitrogen-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), to weaken the interaction between solvent molecules and Na-ions in conventional carbonate-based electrolytes. This, in turn, can lead to an adjustment in the solvation structure. The nitrogen functional groups in the NCQDs play a pivotal role in inducing charge delocalization on the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) due to the electron density difference between carbon and nitrogen atoms. This results in a negatively charged state. Upon introduction into the electrolyte, the negatively charged NCQDs exhibit a high degree of interaction with Na-ion, influencing the solvation environment and promoting the dissolution of the Na salt while simultaneously weakening the interaction between Na-ions and solvent molecules. These phenomena enhance the mobility of Na-ions through the electrolyte, promote the formation of inorganic SEI layers originating from NCQDs and anions, and enable uniform Na deposition on the surface of the Na metal anode (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1). The carbonate-based electrolyte with NCQDs exhibits a high Coulombic efficiency in asymmetric cells, reaching 91 %, and an extended lifespan in symmetric cells, lasting 700 h, even at a high current density of 5 mA cm-2. The high-energy density of the SMB with P2-Na0.67Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 is ultimately achieved using the carbonate-based electrolyte containing NCQDs.

NCQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal process, employing citric acid (CA) and urea as carbon precursors. The synthesis involved the formation of citric acid amide through hydrogen bonding between CA and urea, which transformed the intermediate into NCQDs with abundant oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the edges. In this process, CA played a key role in developing the carbon skeleton due to its low carbonization temperature, while urea introduced nitrogen functional groups throughout the entire process. All comprehensive characterizations of the NCQDs are detailed in Fig. 2 a-c and Fig. S2-S4. In transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2a and Fig. S2), we confirmed a monodisperse shape and a mean size of 2.5 ± 1.1 nm. High-resolution TEM analysis further revealed the crystalline nature of individual NCQDs, with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the [1120] diffraction planes of sp2 clusters [36]. High-resolution XPS spectra provided elemental analysis, thereby verifying the presence of C, O, and N, along with insights into their respective formations. The principal components of NCQDs were carbon (68.2 %), oxygen (16.5 %), and nitrogen (15.3 %), as determined by quantitative element analysis. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 2b was deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N. It was found that the majority of the nitrogen functional groups (more than 88 %) were pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N functional groups. The C 2s and O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra indicated strong interactions between carbon atoms in NCQDs with oxygen and nitrogen atoms (Fig. S2c-d). Further, FT-IR (Fig. 2c) and Raman (Fig. S3) spectra are also presented to ascertain the diverse functional groups on the surface. Through detailed analysis results of their physical and chemical properties, we could elucidate their molecular structure, which consists of multiple graphene layers with substantial N doping in CQDs in the form of graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N (Fig. 2d).

NCQDs exhibit a negative charge, primarily due to the electron-donating properties of heteroatoms like nitrogen and oxygen, which possess lone pair electrons in their functional group. This property is corroborated by the zeta potential value of NCQDs, which measures −44.7 mV (Fig. 2e). Upon the introduction of 0.1 M NaPF6 to the aqueous dispersion, this value decreased to −14.7 mV. This reduction is clear evidence that Na ions are adsorbed onto the surface of negatively charged NCQDs [37]. Because of their substantial negative charge, NCQDs can be colloidally dispersed in carbonate-based electrolytes without any treatment (Fig. 2f and Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvation circumstance in the carbonate-based electrolyte with NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The phenomenon attracting Na ions around negatively charged NCQDs can indeed modulate the solvation circumstance within the electrolyte. We investigated how the solvation circumstance changes when NCQDs are induced into the carbonate-based electrolyte. For this investigation, we selected representatives using different solvents: 1 M NaPF6/DEC, 1 M NaPF6/EC/DEC/FEC (1/1 v/v% with adding 5 wt% FEC, referred to as EDF), and 1 M NaPF6/diglyme, both without and with NCQDs.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted to analyze the interaction between Na ions and electrolyte components (EC, DEC, FEC, diglyme,
PF
6
−
anion), focusing on the binding energies involving NCQDs. The NCQD model was simplified and constructed as a small sp2 -carbon cluster, with the N configurations positioned either in-plane or at the edge (Fig. S6). This structural arrangement was derived from a comprehensive analysis of the available experimental data on NCQDs. Firstly, NCQDs demonstrate the stronger interactions with Na ions at both planar and edge sites compared to any other electrolyte component (Fig. 3 a and Fig. S6-S7). The pyridinic N in NCQDs exhibits the most substantial binding energy with Na-ions, with values of −2.26 eV for the plane and −4.34 eV for the edge, followed by pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and sp2 carbon. Moreover, considering the theoretical stable solvation geometry between Na-ions and solvent molecules in the carbonate and ether electrolyte demonstrates that Na-ions have a stronger interaction with NCQDs (−1.32 to −2.26 eV) compared to carbonate molecules (Na+-(EC)1: −0.34 eV, Na+-(FEC)1: −0.32 eV, Na+-(DEC)2: −0.88 eV), as shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S8-S9. This observation highlights that NCQDs preferentially attract Na-ions in the carbonate-based electrolyte, which disrupts the solvation environment and facilitates the dissociation of salt into Na-ions and anions, thereby altering the solvation structure. In contrast, the binding energy between Na-ions and diglyme is strong, with a value of −2.05 eV (Na+-(diglyme)2) [38]. This proves that Na-ions strongly form the solvation structure with two molecules of diglyme. Consequently, the solvation environment is less affected when NCQDs are introduced into the ether electrolyte than the carbonate electrolyte.

Experimental evidence for adjusting the solvation structure was obtained from nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Firstly, we conducted 23Na NMR measurements on various electrolytes. 23Na NMR is sensitive to the coordinating species in the solvation configuration between solvent molecules and ions [39]. An upfield (shifting to lower value) or downfield (shifting to higher value) peak shift describes the changes in the solvation environment: the upfield shift indicates increased electron density around Na-ions due to either stronger solvent or anion bindings, suggesting that salt aggregates with the solvent molecules in the electrolyte, whereas the downfield shift means the opposite [40]. That is the downfield shift indicates weaker binding of Na-ion with other electrolyte components, leading to a more dissociative anions and generating more free Na-ions [41]. Thus, solvation configurations can be inferred according to the peak position in chemical shift, in which a shift toward more positive values (higher ppm) indicates solvent-separated ion pairs (SSIPs), contact ion pairs (CIPs), and aggregates (AGGs) in order [42–44]. Fig. 3c displays 23Na NMR spectra of raw electrolytes without suspended NCQDs. Both carbonate-based electrolytes have peaks in the upfield region, having more ion pairing and higher electron density around Na-ions. This suggests their solvation structure dominantly comprised of AGGs with CIPs partially (Fig. S10). Conversely, the electrolyte with diglyme shows chemical shift increased to downfield, indicating a different solvation environment characterized by SSIPs and fewer CIPs [38]. This observation is attributed to differences in the solvation capability of each solvent, determined by their dielectric constant and donor number (DN) [45].

This understanding allows us to clarify alterations in the electrolyte solvation structures resulting from the addition of NCQDs. The peak shifting to downfield is observed in carbonated-based electrolytes in Fig. 3d-e, while no such peak shift is observed in the ether-based electrolyte (Fig. S11). These findings imply that the addition of NCQDs influences the solvation structure in only carbonated-based electrolytes, resulting in weaker binding among Na ions, anions and solvents [39,41,42,46]. The presence of NCQDs around the solvation sheath may disrupt the locally ordered structure of the electrolytes and modify electron density around Na-ions. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the peaks became narrower when the NCQDs were incorporated, indicating the faster dynamics of the Na ions in the electrolytes (Fig. S12) [47].

FT-IR further confirms the solvation circumstances consistent with the trend observed in NMR results. The rapid time scale of FT-IR allows the detection of separate absorptions for uncoordinated and coordinated solvents with Na-ions [48]. FT-IR spectra were acquired for each electrolyte between 700 and 1900 cm-1 (Fig. S13). In the case of the electrolyte with diglyme, the CO-C band (ether group) in diglyme coordinates with Na-ions, revealing two peaks between 900 and 1300 cm-1 (Fig. S14a). One peak at 1105 cm-1 is for the CO-C stretching band of free solvent molecules, and another at 1083 cm-1 is for the coordination with Na-ions. Notably, there was no significant difference in the spectra, regardless of the absence and presence of NCQDs in the electrolyte (Fig. S14b). This suggests that the solvation structure already established between diglyme and Na-ions is unaffected by introducing NCQDs. However, for carbonate-based electrolytes, including EC, DEC, and FEC, the spectra were slightly altered after the introduction of NCQDs at both the C = O (carbonyl band, 1600–1800 cm-1) and CO (1100–1350 cm-1) band regions (Fig. 3f-g). For the electrolyte with pure DEC, which has less cooperating Na-ions due to the low dielectric constant (↋=2.8) [49], the FT-IR spectra reveal two main peaks around 1723 and 1741 cm-1 (Fig. 3g). The high-frequency peak corresponds to the C = O stretching mode of free DEC, while the low-frequency peak at 1723 cm-1 indicates solvated DEC with Na ions. Upon adding NCQDs, there appeared to be an increase in the intensity of free C = O and a decrease in the intensity of solvated C = O, accompanied by a change in the peak area [46,50]. Moreover, the relative areas of the deconvoluted peaks corresponding to the coordinated and uncoordinated carbonyl bands were decreased, which is used to estimate the degree of DEC participating in the Na-ion solvation structure (Table S1) [46,48]. These observations suggest a disruption in the interaction between solvated DEC (predominantly referred to as Sol-DEC) and Na-ions by introducing NCQDs, leading to altering the solvation structure. A similar trend was observed in the cases of 1 M NaPF6/EC/DEC/FEC. Minor changes in the peaks at the region for C = O band in DEC was observed (Fig. 3g), and changes also appeared in the CO band for both DEC and EC molecules between 1100 and 1350 cm-1 (Fig. S15a). With the introduction of NCQDs, the deconvoluted peak area for free EC increased, whereas sol-EC decreased (Fig. S15b-c). This implies that the solvation structure is also adjusted even when the solvent mixture of DEC and EC is used, resulting in a weakening of the interaction between solvents and Na ions and increasing free solvents in the electrolyte, which are consistent with DFT calculations and FT-IR results.

In light of the aforementioned investigations, we present simplified solvation structures of both diglyme and carbonate electrolytes upon the introduction of NCQDs in Fig. 3f. The solvation structure in the diglyme electrolyte remains unaltered by the presence of NCQDs. In contrast, in the case of carbonate electrolytes, NCQDs attract Na-ions and disrupt the original solvation structure, thereby weakening the interactions among Na ions, solvent molecules, and anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of Na metal in asymmetric and symmetric cells
ce:section.ce:para:
The impact of the reduced solvation structure in the carbonate electrolyte, adjusted by the introduction of NCQDs, was meticulously examined regarding electrochemical performance. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were conducted using Na||Na symmetric cells with 1 M NaPF6/EC/DEC/FEC (1/1 v/v% with an additional 5 wt% FEC) dispersed without or with NCQDs, referred to as EDF and EDFNC, respectively. The voltage range was set between −0.5 and 3.0 V at a scanning rate of 1 mV s-1 to investigate the Na plating/stripping stability. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the onset voltage of −80 mV for Na plating (i.e., the reduction region at the negative scan) is observed in EDFNC, as quantified by linear extrapolation. This value is notably smaller than that of EDF, which is a value of −97 mV. During the Na plating/stripping process, the cell with EDFNC exhibited a larger redox area compared to the cell with EDF, indicating an increase in the quantity of Na plated/stripped [47]. The exchange current density (i0 ) was fitted with the Tafel plot (Fig. S16), which is a critical parameter associated with ion transfer kinetics at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The i0 value of 0.28 mA cm-2 in EDFNC is over seven times larger than that of EDF (0.039 mA cm-2). These findings demonstrate that the incorporation of NCQDs into the electrolyte not only enables a more reversible Na plating/stripping process, but also enhance Na-ion transport kinetics [51]. The weakened interaction between solvent molecules and Na ions reduces the energy required for Na ions to move within the electrolyte, thereby increasing their ion mobility [52].

To gain further insight into the initial Na nucleation, three-electrode cell tests were conducted. This system allows for the observation of the electrochemical behavior of each electrode individually, thereby providing a more comprehensive understanding of the overall voltage variations. The configuration consists of Cu foil as a working electrode (We), Na foil as both counter (Ce) and reference electrodes (Re) (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4c shows the evolution of the voltage of the individual Na foil (ECe, top) and Cu foil (EWe, middle), as well as the overall voltage profile (EWe-ECe, bottom) during the initial Na deposition process (discharge process). In the discharge process, Na-ions are extracted from the surface of the Na foil, while Na-ions are deposited on the surface of the Cu foil. In case of the cell with EDFNC, the initial voltage of the Na foil (ECe) is lower (375 mV) than that of the cell with EDF (539 mV). This indicates that the energy barrier for extracting Na from the surface of Na in the beginning is reduced in the presence of NCQDs. Meanwhile, the nucleation overpotential on Cu foil, which is the magnitude of the voltage spike at the onset of Na deposition, is similar: 20 mV with EDF and 21 mV with EDFNC. However, the plateau overpotential of the cell with EDFNC (7 mV) had a half value of the cell with EDF (13 mV). This means that although the nucleation process occurs similarly, the addition of a Na adatom to an existing Na nucleus is easier with a lower energy barrier in the cell with EDFNC. Therefore, the overall voltage of the cell with EDFNC exhibited a lower polarization in whole process. In other words, the presence of NCQD enhances the Na ion kinetics on both We and Ce, leading to a reduction in the energy barrier to Na deposition and stripping processes. This can enhance the electrochemical performance of SMBs with EDFNC.

Reversible Na metal plating/stripping processes, influenced by the compatibility of the electrolyte against Na metal, are critical for achieving a stable SMB with a long cycle life. To evaluate the Coulombic efficiency (CE) of Na plating/stripping process, which is a measure of reversibility and compatibility, Na||Cu cells were assembled and tested at a high current density of 1 mA cm-2 using the modified Aurbach's method [53]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the EDFNC with the attenuated solvation structure exhibited a higher average Coulombic efficiency (CE) up to 91 % for 20 cycles compared to the cell with EDF (81 %). The effectiveness of EDFNC is even more pronounced during extended cycling over 100 cycles (Fig. S17), where the CE was 92 %. Such performance indicates the excellent reversibility and compatibility of the Na metal with EDFNC. Furthermore, Na||Na symmetric cells were used to investigate the long-term stability. Fig. 4e and Fig. S18-S19 show the voltage profiles of the symmetric cells with EDF and EDFNC at varying current densities at a fixed area capacity of 0.5 mA h cm-2. The cell with EDFNC exhibited a stable cycling performance up to 700 h at a current density of 0.5 mA cm-2. In contrast, the symmetric cell in EDF showed an obvious voltage fluctuation and even experienced short-circuiting within 10 h (Fig. 4e). The enhancement in cyclability was also confirmed by varying the concentrations of NCQDs in the electrolyte (Fig. S18). The improved cyclability in the cell with EDFNC was also observed under extreme operating conditions of 2.5 and 5 mA cm-2 with the limited capacity of 0.5 mA h cm-2 (Fig. S19). In contrast, the Na||Na symmetric cells with EDF were short-circuiting within 10 h under the same conditions. The superior performance of the cell with EDFNC exceeds that of previously reported Na anodes with carbonate-based electrolytes, as summarized in Fig. 4f and Table S2.

Fig. 5 depicts the role of NCQD in the carbonate-based electrolyte with regard to the Na ion deposition process. The deposition of Na ions moved from Na foil onto the Cu foil was observed in the Na||Cu half-cell with EDF or EDFNC (Fig. 5a). First, Sand's time for each the system was determined by depositing Na metal at a high current density of 10 mA cm-2 (Fig. 5b and Fig. S20). Sand's time is defined as the specific moment at which the ion concentration drops to zero on the electrode surface at currents exceeding the diffusion limit. At Sand's time, a large number of metal ions rapidly deposit on the electrode, leading to short circuits of the cell. The cell with EDF exhibited Sand's time of approximately 940 s, whereas the cell with EDFNC was prolonged of around 3000 s, more than three times longer. This result indicates a reduced probability of Na dendrite-induced short circuits. Additionally, Na ion diffusion coefficients (
D
N
a
+
) for both electrolytes were calculated using the formula for Sand's time, which is described in Fig. S21 and Table S1 [54].
D
N
a
+
for EDFNC is 2.13 × 10–11 cm2 s-1, nearly twice that of the EDF, which is 7.05 × 10–12 cm2 s-1. Therefore, these results support that NCQD as an additive can facilitate Na ion movement in the carbonate-based electrolyte, thereby inhibiting Na dendrite growth and consequently prolonging the lifespan of Na metal cells.

The morphologies of the deposited Na metal in the presence of NCQDs exhibit uniform, dendrite-free deposition of highly packed Na. Fig. 5c-h and Fig. S22 display SEM images of Na metal deposited onto the Cu foil for EDF and EDFNC, at a current density of 0.5 mA cm-2 and fixed capacities of 1, 3, and 6 mA h cm-2. When the EDF electrolyte was used, the deposited Na showed a highly heterogeneous structure with numerous typical chunks and dendrites (Fig. 5c-e and Fig. S22a-c). As the capacity increased, the irregular Na structure persisted with the spongy and less dense structure. This dendrite formation is a common phenomenon when cells operate above certain current density thresholds in carbonate electrolytes for Li or Na metal [55]. In contrast, the EDFNC electrolyte results in the formation of dense and dendrite-free sodium, composed of highly packed sodium. The high magnification images demonstrate the presence of highly dense structures even the deposited Na capacity increased (Fig. 5f-h). The low-magnification images indicate the dendrite-free Na growth across the Cu substrate (Fig. S22d-f).

The morphology of Na deposition provides indirect evidence reflecting the uniformity of SEI layers. To elucidate the continuous, uniform, and dendrite-free growth of Na, the properties of SEI layers on the surface of Cu foil were analyzed by XPS measurements after 10 cycles with EDF and EDFNC electrolytes. The electrodes were etched for 30 s, resulting in an etched depth of 2–3 nm. The results for the elements including carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and phosphorus (P) are shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S23-S24. In the case of the EDF electrolyte, the presence of carbon-containing species in the SEI is more dominant than in EDFNC (28 % vs. 23 % for the outer layer) (Fig. 6a-b). More carbonyl (CO) and carboxyl (C = O) species in EDF were detected, accompanied by the Na carbonate (Na2CO3) (Fig. S20a-c, ESI†), which are typical from carbonate-based electrolytes. At the initial stage of SEI formation, Na-ions solvated by EC molecules facilitates a single-electron reduction of carbonate solvents, resulting in Na ethylene decarbonate (NaO₂COC₂H₄-OCO₂Na, NEDC), which accumulates and transforms into an amorphous organic/polymer layer. Due to its poor hydrolytic stability, NEDC undergoes further reaction to form Na₂CO₃, ethylene, and CO₂ in the presence of trace water or acid, which are always present in the electrolyte. This resulted in destabilization of the SEI layer, leading to the formation of random Na dendrites [56]. In contrast, the reduced content of CO, C = O, and Na₂CO₃ in the EDFNC electrolyte indicates a diminished tendency for solvent decomposition relative to that observed in EDF, which in turn stabilizes the SEI layer ((Fig. S20d-f). Additionally, the SEI layer in the EDFNC electrolyte consists of various inorganic compounds (Na2O, NaF, Na-phosphate (Na-PO4 3-), N-containing species), which make it more robust and resistant to deformation (Fig. 6c and Fig. S24). These compounds contribute to enhanced stability against further reduction, facilitating the migration of Na-ions within the electrolyte and enabling dendrite-free Na plating under the robust SEI layer. The SEI layer in the EDFNC is richer in inorganic components compared to the EDF, which is richer in organic species, as displayed in Fig. 6d. These properties influence SEI morphologies, as shown Fig. S25 [57]. The SEI layer in the EDF electrolyte was fluffy and porous after the plating/stripping process for 100th cycle, along with many residues. In contrast, the EDFNC formed the SEI layers that were thin and dense. This difference is attributed to the content of organic components, which typically possess long organic chains that hinder them forming compact packing of the SEI components. This hypothesis is also supported by lower ionic conductivity of Na2CO3 (5.7 × 10–23 S cm-1 at 25 °C) and its tendency to deform more easily due to lower mechanical strength (Young's modulus: 31.9 and 11.5 GPa) compared to Na2O (Ion conductivity: 1.5 × 10–12 S cm-1 at 25 °C, Young's modulus: 61.8 and 67.4 GPa) [58].

Overall, the introduction of NCQDs into the carbonate-based electrolyte weakens the interaction between Na ions and solvent molecules, resulting in reduced decomposition of solvent molecules. The SEI layer formed in this system exhibits a higher concentration of inorganic species and a lower concentration of organic species. This type of SEI layer is characterized by high ion conductivity and mechanical strength, attributed to the inorganic species present, leading to a dense and robust SEI structure. Consequently, the Na ion plating process under this SEI layer is more stabilized than without NCQDs, promoting a dendrite-free system and enhancing electrochemical performance.

Lastly, we evaluated the feasibility of the EDFNC electrolyte in practical applications. Full cells were assembled by pairing with P2-NNM or NVP as cathodes with Na metal as anodes, along with the EDF or EDFNC electrolytes. The cathodes for full cells were synthesized and characterized in advance (Fig. S26). P2-NNM is attractive for SMBs due to its stability in air, high working voltage of ∼3.8 V vs. Na/Na+, and high practical capacity originated from high redox potential of Ni2+/Ni3+/Ni4+ [59].In this study, high-loading P2-NNM electrodes (12∼14 mg cm-2) were employed in the following studies under controlled operating voltage conditions. Fig. 7
a shows the prolonged cycling stability of SMB with the EDFNC electrolyte in comparison to the EDF electrolyte at a current density of 1 C, which is 80 mAh g-1, between 2.0 and 4.0 V. The EDFNC electrolyte demonstrated a capacity retention of 93 % capacity with an average CE of 99.99 % after 110 cycles, whereas EDF retained 85 % with the average CE of 99.72 %. The high CE in the cell with the EDFNC indicates a reduction in parasitic side reactions on the Na metal anode during charging (plating) and discharging (stripping). This is corroborated by the reduction in resistance observed in the cell (Fig. S27). According to the detailed voltage profiles (Fig. 7b and Fig. S28), the full cell using the EDFNC exhibits no voltage polarization throughout the cycling process. Upon increasing the current density to 2 C, the cell with the EDFNC electrolyte exhibited consistent cyclability for 200 cycles, retaining a capacity of 76 % (Fig. 7c). Furthermore, full cells were cycled in the voltage range of 2.0–4.3 V to verify effects of the EDFNC electrolyte at the high operating voltage (Fig. 7d-e). It is noteworthy that the cell stability improved in the EDFNC electrolyte, resulting in a capacity retention of 63 % after 110 cycles with the average CE of 99.31 %, compared to the EDF electrolyte, which retained 27 % with an average CE of 98.72 %. In the case of cell paired with NVP (mass loading: ∼8.2 mg cm-2), the EDFNC electrolyte also enhanced electrochemical properties. It delivered low voltage polarization, demonstrated cycle stability for 500 cycles at a current density of 5 C (1 C = 90 mA g-1), and exhibited low resistance compared to the cell with the EDF electrolyte (Fig. S29).

All of these results clearly confirm that the EDFNC electrolyte with NCQDs, which adjust the solvation structure to weaken the interaction between solvent molecules and Na-ions, can be an effective strategy for building practical SMBs with high performance.

In summary, we present a carbonate-based electrolyte with a weak solvation structure for practical SMBs by adding NCQDs. The negatively charged state of NCQDs interacts with Na ions, weakening the interaction between solvent molecules and Na-ions. This electrolyte was investigated in detail as proof of concept. We found that the addition of NCQD into the carbonate-based electrolyte results in improved CE, reduced Na nucleation overpotential, and prolonged plating/stripping performance up to 700 h. Moreover, the NCQD additive not only plays a critical role in enhancing Na ion mobility in the electrolyte but also induces the generation of a robust and stable SEI layer enriched with inorganic constituents. The designed electrolyte by NCQD (EDFNC) allows for the sustainable operation of practical SMBs with various cathodes, confirming that the EDFNC electrolyte is not limited to specific cathode materials. We expect the proposed electrolyte with NCQD to contribute to a deeper understanding of (1) adjusting the Na ion solvation environment using negatively or positively charged materials in electrolytes, (2) a SEI layer consisted of inorganics for Na metal, (3) developing a facile and effective strategy for practical electrolytes, and (4) manipulating the salt/solvent/additive chemistry of the electrolytes.

Synthesis of NCQDs: NCQDs were prepared by a solvothermal reaction. A solution containing 3 mmol of citric acid (purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) and 10 mmol of urea (purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) as carbon sources were prepared by dissolving them in 10 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich). The total solution was placed in a sealed Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was mixed with an excess of ethyl acetate to precipitate the final product. The resulting black precipitate was washed with a mixture of ethyl acetate and methanol and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven. A detailed description of this method can be found in referenced paper [60].

Preparation of Electrolytes with NCQDs: The electrolytes used in this study consisted of a 1 M solution of Na hexafluorophosphate (NaPF6) in the respective solvents. Two solvent compositions were tested: a mixture of diethyl carbonate (DEC)/ethylene carbonate (EC) (1/1, v/v ratio) with fluorine ethylene carbonate (FEC) of 5 wt% or diethylene glycol dimethyl ether (diglyme). All chemicals used in the system were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Prior to use, the salt was dried under vacuum at 80 °C overnight and then transferred into an Ar-filled glove box (O2 and H2O <1 ppm).

To prepare the electrolytes containing NCQDs, the NCQDs were added to pre-made electrolytes in a glove box. The concentration NCQDs in the electrolytes was adjusted to 0.1, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg mL-1. The electrolytes with NCQDs were stirred for more one day to ensure proper dispersion before being used in experiments. All electrolytes were carefully prepared and stored inside an Ar-filled glove box to maintain an inert atmosphere.

Synthesis of Cathodes: (P2-Na0.67Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 (P2-NNM)) To synthesize P-NNM, a modified solid-state reaction was employed according to previous literature [61]. All chemicals used in the synthesis were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The precursor materials, namely sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), nickel oxide (NiO2), and manganese dioxide (MnO2), were mixed in a high-energy ball milling container for 1 hr. The mixing was done to achieve an approximate stoichiometric ratio required for P2-NNM. Subsequently, the resulting power mixture was annealed in a cubic furnace at 900 °C for 24 hrs under air atmosphere. After the process, the sample was naturally cooled down to room temperature. Finally, the obtained P-NNM product was manually grounded in a mortar to ensure uniformity of the material.

(Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVP)) A solution was prepared by dissolving NaH₂PO₄ (3.75 mmol), NH₄VO₃ (2.5 mmol), citric acid (6.25 mmol), and 0.6 g of polyethylene oxide (weight average molecular weight = 1000 kg/mol) in 30 mL of distilled water. After stirring for 12 hrs, the homogeneous precursor solution was transferred to a syringe and wet-spun in a 200 mL THF bath. The as-collected fibers were then subjected to calcination at 500 °C for 2 hrs and 800 °C for 10 hrs under an Ar atmosphere, resulting in the formation of Na₃V₂(PO₄)₃.

Electrochemical Measurements: All the cells, including asymmetric, symmetric and full cells, were assembled using 2032-type coin cells. The assembly process took place inside an Ar-filled glove (O2 and H2O < 1 ppm) to maintain a controlled atmosphere. Polypropylene film (PP, Celgard 2400) and glass microfiber filter (Whatman, GF/A) separators were used in the cells. The electrolyte volume used in each cell, both without and with NCQDs, were approximately 100 μL. The specific types of electrolytes used in each cell are described in the main text. The cell tests were conducted at a constant temperature of 25 °C. The mentioned conditions were consistent across all experiments, unless stated otherwise. For assembling symmetric cells used in galvanostatic polarization and cycling performance tests, Na metal was employed as both the working and counter electrodes. Na metal (≥ 99.0 %, Sigma-Aldrich) was manually pressed by pressing, rolling and cutting into round pieces with a diameter of either 10 mm or 16 mm and a thickness of 200 μm. Asymmetric cells were constructed using Cu foil as a working electrode and Na metal as a counter electrode. These cells were used for testing the Coulombic efficiency of the Na stripping/plating process. A three-electrode cell designed from Wellcos Corporation (HS flat cell) was utilized, with Na metal serving as a reference and a counter electrode, and Cu foil as a working electrode. To evaluate the practical performance in full cells, P2-NNM with a loading density of approximately 7 mg cm-2 was employed as the cathode material (diameter of 14 mm). P2-NNM, poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), and super-P carbon black were mixed in a weight ratio of 8:1:1, using N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone as the solvent. The resulting slurry was coated onto Al foil and dried in a vacuum oven at 150 °C overnight. The specific capacity, energy, and power density calculations were based on the total mass of P2-NNM. All the cells were tested at different current densities with a battery test system (WBS3000, Wonatech). Cyclic voltammograms (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured using a multichannel potentiostat (VSP-300, Bio-Logic) in a constant-temperature oven set at 25 °C. EIS were obtained and analyzed in a frequency range of 10–3 to 106 Hz, using an amplitude of 10 mV.

Materials Characterization: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a JEM-2200FS field emission electron microscope (JEOL) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was implemented with ESCALAB 2500 (Thermo Scientific). The morphology of plated Na on Cu foil and P-2NNM was observed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM 7401F of JEOL). To prevent unwanted reactions with oxygen and moisture, samples were transferred in a sealed container with Ar gas and analyzed by SEM and XPS measurements. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on an Agilent Cary 600 FTIR spectrometer in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm-1. For the FTIR analysis of NCQDs, a mixture of KBr and NCQDs in a weight ratio of 99: 1 was prepared. To confirm the solvation of the electrolytes, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurements were performed using a Bruker Avance Ⅲ Ascend 600 instrument. 23Na NMR shift was referenced to 1 M NaCl solution in D2O. A reference compound of the chemical shift for 23Na NMR was 1 M NaCl in D2O. The crystal structure of synthesized P2-NNM was characterized by X-ray Diffractometer (SmartLab, Rigaku).

Computational Details: Spin-polarize density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the DMol3 program [62,63]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional was used to describe the exchange-correlation energy [64]. Core electrons were explicitly treated as all electrons, incorporating relativistic effects. The DNP 4.4 atomic orbital basis set was employed. Self-consistent field calculations were performed with a convergence criterion of 1.0 × 10–6 and a smearing value of 0.005 Ha. The convergence criteria for geometry optimization were set to 1.0 × 10–5 Ha for energy, 0.002 Ha/Å for force, and 0.005 Å for displacement, respectively. The conductor-like screening model (COSMO) was used to model the solvation environment of electrolytes with a 1:1 ratio of EC and DEC by applying dielectric constant of 29.96 [65,66]. For each optimized geometry of the NCQD with a bonded ion (e.g., Na+), the binding energy was calculated using the formula: E NCQD–ion - E NCQD - E ion.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version.
